Failure Prediction Methodology for ESP and Operational Behavior
L. E. Cardona, Bond Energy, Bogotá, Colombia; P. J. Vivas Sanchez, Sensia Global, Bogotá, Colombia; B. Joya,
Ecopetrol, Bogotá, Colombia
Copyright 2023, Society of Petroleum Engineers DOI 10.2118/213140-MS
This paper was prepared for presentation at the SPE Latin American and Caribbean Petroleum Engineering Conference held in Port of Spain, Trinidad and Tobago,
14 - 15 June 2023.
This paper was selected for presentation by an SPE program committee following review of information contained in an abstract submitted by the author(s). Contents

Abstract
One of the most active challenges in the production life cycle is the ability to predict failure to avoid
loss of production and increase deferred production. This simple question affects many areas of operation
such as maintenance, supply, production, and planning. The digital transformation enables us to disrupt the
conventional mode of operation and create space for such solutions.
This project aims to improve operational decision making for the logistics of maintenance planning in
critical wells with ESP lifting systems of an oil field in Colombia's Upper Magdalena Valley basin. This
enhancement is provided by combining Machine Learning techniques for the prediction of downhole pump
failures by various agents. This project's scope is predictive failures with Analytics insight. The periodicity
of failures and the impact of each of these, measured in terms of deferred production, define the criticality
of the wells.
The project was developed by conducting an analysis of the state of the art in this type of prediction both
within and outside the industry. Following this, historical data is collected, the data to be analyzed is selected
and prepared (including sample data balancing), and the data is treated with Python. Following that, the
objective is defined based on the criticality of the potential failures, and machine learning algorithms are
used to determine the variables with the greatest predictive potential, and then a classification algorithm is
run to predict with enough time to support decision making (hours before the event). The outcome of these
processes is validated, first with information from the training data and then with hidden data.
This paper discusses the advantages of predicting failures in ESP pumps with performance greater than
60%. This is accomplished in two steps, the first of which is determining which variables contribute the most
to the prediction of background failures. This analysis produced insights for field decision-making, assisting
those who monitor more than 50 variables. The second step took the selected variables and delivered to
maintenance planners an algorithm capable of predicting pump failures with high accuracy in enough time
to perform preventive maintenance, reducing production deferrals of high-potential wells.
This project helps to increase the use of these techniques in Latin America, as well as the development
of future research in the field of predictive and prescriptive analytics and the expansion of periods that
lead to better tactical decision making. This solution can be extended to other fields with similar failure
characteristics, allowing it to contribute to production optimization in a short period.

Introduction
Over 90% of producer wells in the world require an artificial lift system (ALS). (VON FLATERN, 2018)
mentions the importance of ALS to produce well that have no energy enough to take crude to the surface
or as a way to increase initial production to improve the financial return. According to (Long Peng et al.,
2021), electrical submersible pumps (ESP) are one of the most widely employed ALS, accounting for over
60% of the global oil production. This is also the ALS with the widest production range, with availability
to work different casing diameters and a financial benefit up to 90% based on volumetric efficiency.
The electro submersible pumping system is composed of both subsurface and surface equipment that
allow the proper functioning of the system.
The basic subsurface components of an electro submersible pumping system consist of motor, protector,
intake or pump inlet and power cable, as well as some accessories such as gas separators, check and bleeder
valves and centralizers among others, which are added according to the design, well conditions and operation
requirements.
The standard ESP used presents several economic and technical obstacles, due to the high wear in the
internal components of the pumps and their short range of operating efficiency with respect to the required
production. This is the result of a low resistance of the motors to temperatures higher than 400°F generating
overheating, high tendency to Downthrust and Upthrust due to inefficient designs, abrasive environments
with solids and sands content higher than 6%, as well as high well deviations, designs with more rotors for
higher power and high thrust and loads on the pump shaft that require more robust protectors and seals.
These obstacles result in constant maintenance services and workover operations, investment in more
pump stages to meet lifting requirements and high-energy consumption, which significantly increase
production costs for the company.
In general terms, the damages that can be generated in the equipment of this lifting system are:
– Excessive load for an extended period.
– Leaks in the seal or in the section of the protectors.
– Wear of the equipment by insufficient amount of fluid in movement, high temperature, corrosion and
abrasion, by the conditions of the well.
– Failure due to careless or faulty installation.
– Motor controller failures,
– Imperfections in the equipment due to bad design or inadequate handling.
– Defective electrical system.
– Clogging of stages due to deposition of displaced material.
– Wear of the seals due to vibration of the pump.
– Mechanical damage of the cable during the assembly run.
The failures in the ESP system are classified based on the following parameters:
– Mechanical type failures
– Electrical type failures
– Well failures
Machine Learning Approach
ESP's are one of the most expensive equipment in artificial lift systems. That is why a failure in this kind
of pumps means a huge production deferred and a big investment to restore production for operators. The
large costs and the availability to get information from ESP have made some operators to focus on the
development of some techniques to improve the optimization systems and the surveillance of the parameters
and variables of sensors of the pump. These techniques have evolved to the use of data science and machine

Conclusions and Results
It is recommended to the operating company to review the process of generation and storage of information,
because the error of the JSON files may be increasing, leaving the information in the data lake useless.
The data sample provided by the company allows understanding the behavior of the ESP pumps.
It is recommended to the company to validate the storage processes from the source to the datalake, in
order to ensure the integrity of the data for further analysis.
The involvement of the user area is important in data analysis as it allows correlating the data with the
operational reality, bringing data science closer to the operation.
Data exploration through the visualization of parameters in time series and the association matrix allows
to understand the relationships of the parameters in an analytical way.
The collinearity matrix allows an understanding of the possible relationships that exist between the data
and the objective function in a visual and quick way.
Given the classification generated with the PCA, it is possible to identify three components in which the
pump failure is predicted a few hours in advance and the failure moments are identified.
The model is configured so that it can be implemented in production in such a way that it could identify
the prediction of failures in real time.
